  • CLI Reference — Every command documented
  • Architecture — How it all fits together

docs.title: First Steps
docs.text: This walkthrough takes you from a fresh install to a fully operational encrypted vault in under five minutes. By the end you'll know how to create, search, and manage secrets with APM.

docs.title: Step 1: Run Setup
docs.text:
Run the setup command to create and configure your vault:

pm setup

You'll be prompted for:

  1. Master Password — Choose a strong passphrase (12+ characters recommended). This password is never stored — it's used to derive encryption keys via Argon2id.

  2. Security Profile — APM detects your system hardware and recommends a profile:

    Profile Memory Iterations Parallelism Best For Standard 64 MB 3 2 Most systems Hardened 256 MB 5 4 ≥8 GB RAM, ≥4 cores Paranoid 512 MB 6 4 ≥16 GB RAM, ≥8 cores Legacy PBKDF2 600k 1 Compatibility only

  3. Encryption Method — For new vaults, APM asks which AEAD cipher to use:

    Method Notes aes-gcm Default compatibility-oriented choice xchacha20-poly1305 Requires a 24-byte nonce; selectable in setup

  4. Optional setup tasks — Spaces, plugins, and cloud sync can all be configured in the same guided flow.

What just happened?

APM created an encrypted vault file (default: vault.dat beside the binary unless APM_VAULT_PATH is set). The file uses the Vault V4 format — an APMVAULT header followed by encrypted JSON, protected by the selected AEAD cipher and HMAC-SHA256.

docs.title: Step 2: Unlock Your Vault
docs.text:
APM uses explicit sessions — the vault is encrypted at rest and must be unlocked before any sensitive operation:

pm unlock

You'll enter your master password and choose:

  • Session duration (default: 30 minutes)
  • Inactivity timeout (default: 10 minutes)
  • Read-only mode (optional — prevents writes)

How Sessions Work

pm unlock decrypts the vault in memory and creates a session file tied to your terminal. All sensitive commands check for an active session. The session auto-expires after the configured duration or inactivity timeout.

Sessions are shell-scoped — each terminal can have its own independent session via the APM_SESSION_ID environment variable.

docs.title: Step 3: Add Your First Entry
docs.text:
pm add

APM presents an interactive menu of 25+ secret types:

 1. Password          8. API Key         15. CI/CD Secret
 2. TOTP              9. Token           16. Secure Note
 3. Government ID    10. SSH Key         17. Recovery Codes
 4. Medical Record   11. SSH Config      18. Certificate
 5. Travel Info      12. Cloud Creds     19. Banking
 6. Contact          13. Kubernetes      20. Document
 7. Wi-Fi            14. Docker          21. Software License
                                         22. Legal Contract

Select a type and fill in the structured fields. For example, adding a password:

Type: 1 (Password)
Account: github.com
Username: aarav
Password: ********** (hidden input)
Space: Work (optional)

Password Generation

Use pm gen to generate a high-entropy password before adding it:

pm gen
# Output: K$7mP!2qX#9nL@4vR  (copied to clipboard)

docs.title: Step 4: Search and Retrieve Entries
docs.text:
pm get github

APM uses fuzzy search to find matching entries across all types. Results are displayed in an interactive browser where you can:

  • Press Enter to view full details
  • Press C to copy the password to clipboard
  • Press E to edit the entry
  • Press D to delete the entry
  • Press Q for quicklook (preview notes, photos)
  • Press M to view metadata (created, last accessed, trust score)

Safe by Default

Sensitive fields like passwords are hidden by default. Use the --show-pass flag or press S in the interactive view to reveal them:

pm get github --show-pass

docs.title: Step 5: Set Up TOTP (Two-Factor Authentication)
docs.text:
Add a TOTP entry with pm add, then access your 2FA codes:

# Interactive TOTP list with live countdown
pm totp

# Copy a specific code directly
pm totp github

The interactive TOTP view shows live codes with countdown timers and supports drag-to-reorder for your most-used accounts.

docs.title: Step 6: Lock Your Vault
docs.text:
When you're done working, lock the vault to wipe all decrypted data from memory:

pm lock

Warning

Always lock your vault when stepping away. While sessions auto-expire, explicitly locking is the safest practice.

docs.title: Step 7: Set Up Cloud Sync (Optional)
docs.text:
Sync your encrypted vault across devices using any supported provider:

Google DriveGitHubDropbox 
pm cloud init gdrive
# Opens browser for OAuth consent

pm cloud init github
# Enter your Personal Access Token and repo name

pm cloud init dropbox
# Opens browser for OAuth consent

Then sync:

pm cloud sync

Info

Your master password never leaves your machine. Only the encrypted vault blob is uploaded. Cloud providers cannot see your entries.

docs.title: Step 8: Enable Autofill (Windows Only)
docs.text:
If you're on Windows, start the autofill daemon:

pm autocomplete enable   # Register autostart + start daemon
pm unlock                # Unlock the daemon's vault state

Now press Ctrl+Shift+L on any login form and APM will detect the context and inject your credentials — no browser extension needed.

docs.title: Quick Reference Card
docs.text: Task Command Run setup pm setup Unlock vault pm unlock Lock vault pm lock Add an entry pm add Search entries pm get [query] Edit an entry pm get [query] then press e Delete an entry pm get [query] then press d Generate password pm gen View TOTP codes pm totp Sync to cloud pm cloud sync Check vault health pm health View trust scores pm trust View audit log pm audit System info pm info Crypto info pm cinfo Self-update pm update

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text: APM provides multiple ways to get assistance, from built-in documentation to community support.

docs.title: Inline Help
docs.text:
Every command and subcommand supports the --help flag:

# Top-level help
pm --help

# Command-specific help
pm add --help
pm cloud --help
pm cloud init --help
pm mcp --help
pm auth --help
pm plugins --help

This displays usage syntax, available flags, and a brief description of each command.

docs.title: System Information
docs.text:
View your current APM installation details:

pm info

This displays:

  • APM version
  • Go version and build info
  • OS and architecture
  • Vault file path
  • Install directory

For cryptographic parameters of your vault:

pm cinfo

This shows:

  • Active encryption profile
  • Active cipher
  • Argon2id parameters (memory, time, parallelism)
  • Nonce size
  • Vault format version

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text:
If you encounter a bug or have a feature request:

  1. Check existing issues: github.com/aaravmaloo/apm/issues
  2. Open a new issue with:
    • APM version (pm info)
    • OS and architecture
    • Steps to reproduce
    • Expected vs. actual behavior

Security Vulnerabilities

If you discover a security vulnerability, do not open a public issue. Instead, email aaravmaloo06@gmail.com directly with details of the vulnerability.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text:
• Primary Maintainer: Aarav Maloo
  • Security Alerts: aaravmaloo06@gmail.com
  • GitHub: aaravmaloo/apm

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: APM can be installed via one-line scripts, pre-built binaries, or by building from source.

docs.title: One-Line Install (Recommended)
docs.text:
macOS / LinuxWindows PowerShell 
curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaravmaloo/apm/master/scripts/install.sh | bash

Install layout:

Platform Binary location Symlink macOS /usr/local/opt/apm/apm /usr/local/bin/apm → /usr/local/opt/apm/apm Linux /opt/apm/apm /usr/local/bin/apm → /opt/apm/apm 
Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process -Force
iwr https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aaravmaloo/apm/master/scripts/install.ps1 -UseBasicParsing | iex

Install layout:

  • Binary: %LOCALAPPDATA%\pm\apm.exe
  • The installer appends the directory to your user PATH if not already present

After installation, verify with:

pm info

docs.title: Pre-built Binaries
docs.text:
Pre-built binaries for Windows, macOS, and Linux are available for each stable release:

  1. Go to the Releases page
  2. Download the binary for your OS and architecture
  3. Place it in a directory on your PATH
  4. Verify with pm info

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Go 1.21 or later
  • Git
  • Windows, macOS, or Linux

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/aaravmaloo/apm.git
cd apm

# Build the binary
go build -o pm main.go

# Verify the build
./pm info

# Run the guided setup flow
./pm setup

Tip

On Windows, the output binary will be pm.exe. You can move it to a directory on your PATH for global access.

docs.title: Preview and Beta Builds
docs.text:
Pre-built binaries for preview, beta, and canary builds are available from the Builds page.

Warning

Preview and beta builds may be unstable and could potentially corrupt your vault. These builds are intended for developers and testers only. Do not use them for production vaults.

The APM version system follows these stages (e.g., for a v8 release):

Stage Tag Stability Canary can-8 Experimental Beta beta-8 Testing Pre-release pre-8 Near-stable Release v8 Stable

docs.title: Updating
docs.text:
APM includes a self-update mechanism:

pm update

This checks for the latest release on GitHub and downloads/replaces the binary automatically. You can also force a check with:

pm update --force

docs.title: Uninstalling
docs.text:
macOS / LinuxWindows 
# Remove the binary and symlink
sudo rm -f /usr/local/bin/apm
sudo rm -rf /usr/local/opt/apm   # macOS
sudo rm -rf /opt/apm              # Linux

# Optionally remove config and vault data
rm -rf ~/.config/apm

# Remove the binary directory
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\pm"

# Remove from PATH (manual step in System Properties → Environment Variables)

# Optionally remove config data
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force "$env:APPDATA\apm"

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Once installed, proceed to First Steps to run setup and start managing secrets.

docs.title: Using .apmignore
docs.text: The .apmignore file controls what APM excludes from cloud upload payloads. It's processed before encryption and upload during pm cloud sync, allowing you to maintain local-only data while still syncing the rest of your vault.

docs.title: File Location
docs.text:
Place .apmignore in one of these locations (searched in order):

  1. Same directory as your vault file (most common)
  2. Current working directory when running pm cloud sync

APM loads the nearest applicable file.

docs.title: [spaces] — Ignore Entire Spaces
docs.text:
Exclude whole spaces from cloud uploads:

[spaces]
private
temp
archive_*

Supports glob wildcards (* matches any characters).

docs.title: [entries] — Ignore Specific Entries
docs.text:
Exclude entries using space:type:name patterns:

[entries]
work:password:legacy_admin
*:notes:*draft*
personal:api_key:test_*
*:ssh_key:*
Component Description Wildcard Support space Space name * for any type Entry type * for any name Entry name * for any

Type Names

Use lowercase type identifiers: password, totp, token, notes, api_key, ssh_key, ssh_config, wifi, gov_id, medical, travel, contact, cloud_creds, k8s, docker, cicd, recovery_codes, certificate, banking, document, software_license, legal, photo, audio, video.

docs.title: [cloud-specific-ignore] — Provider-Specific Rules
docs.text:
Exclude entries only for a specific provider:

[cloud-specific-ignore]
dropbox:work:password:legacy_admin
github:*:ssh_key:*
gdrive:personal:notes:*journal*

Format: provider:space:type:name

Supported providers: gdrive, github, dropbox

docs.title: [vocab] — Ignore Vocabulary Words
docs.text:
Exclude specific words from the vocabulary index during cloud exports:

[vocab]
internal_project_name
secret_codename

docs.title: [misc] — Miscellaneous Flags
docs.text:
[misc]
ignore:vocab
Flag Effect ignore:vocab Strip the entire compressed vocabulary from uploads

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
# ============================================
# .apmignore — APM Cloud Upload Filter
# ============================================

# Spaces to never upload
[spaces]
private
temp_*

# Entries to exclude from all providers
[entries]
work:password:local_dev_db
*:notes:*scratch*
personal:ssh_key:id_ed25519_local

# Provider-specific exclusions
[cloud-specific-ignore]
dropbox:work:password:legacy_admin
github:*:document:*confidential*

# Vocabulary words to exclude
[vocab]
internal_project_alpha

# Strip the entire vocabulary from uploads
[misc]
ignore:vocab

docs.title: Pattern Matching Rules
docs.text:
• Patterns are case-insensitive for space and type matching
  • Entry names are case-sensitive
  • * matches zero or more characters (glob-style)
  • Comments start with # and are ignored
  • Inline comments after entries are supported (after #)
  • Empty lines are skipped

docs.title: How Filtering Works
docs.text:
When you run pm cloud sync:

  1. APM loads and parses .apmignore
  2. A filtered copy of the vault is created in memory
  3. Entries matching ignore rules are removed from the copy
  4. If ignore:vocab is set, the vocabulary blob is stripped
  5. The filtered vault is encrypted and uploaded

Local Vault Unaffected

Filtering only affects the upload payload. Your local vault file is never modified by .apmignore. All ignored entries remain safely stored locally.

docs.title: Debugging
docs.text:
To verify what your .apmignore is filtering, check the audit log after a sync:

pm audit

Sync events include details about which provider received the upload and how many entries were in the filtered payload.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Cloud Synchronization — Full sync guide
  • .apmignore Reference — Format specification

docs.title: Cloud Synchronization
docs.text: APM syncs your encrypted vault to cloud providers so you can access credentials across all your trusted devices. Your master password never leaves your machine — only encrypted vault blobs are uploaded.

docs.title: Supported Providers
docs.text: Feature Google Drive GitHub Dropbox Authentication OAuth2 (PKCE) Personal Access Token OAuth2 (PKCE) Storage Location Application Data Folder Private Repository Application Folder Setup Speed Instant (browser OAuth) Manual (token + repo) Instant (browser OAuth) Privacy High (isolated from files) Exceptional (git history) High (isolated from files) Version History Limited (native) Comprehensive (git commits) Limited (native) Best For Mobile, fast sync Developers, power users Cross-platform

docs.title: Google Drive
docs.text:
pm cloud init gdrive

This opens your browser for Google OAuth consent. APM uses the Application Data folder, which is isolated from your regular Drive files — you won't see the vault blob in your Drive UI.

docs.title: GitHub
docs.text:
pm cloud init github

You'll need to provide:

  1. A Personal Access Token (PAT) with repo scope
  2. A private repository name (e.g., my-vault-backup)

GitHub sync stores the vault as a file in the repo, giving you comprehensive version history via git commits.

docs.title: Dropbox
docs.text:
pm cloud init dropbox

This opens your browser for Dropbox OAuth consent. APM uses the Application folder, isolated from your regular Dropbox files.

docs.title: All Providers At Once
docs.text:
pm cloud init all

Initializes Google Drive, GitHub, and Dropbox in sequence.

docs.title: Manual Sync
docs.text:
pm cloud sync

This uploads your current vault to all configured providers. The vault is filtered through .apmignore before encryption and upload.

docs.title: Downloading
docs.text:
pm cloud get

Downloads the remote vault blob. You'll be prompted to choose how to handle it:

  1. Overwrite local — Replace your local vault with the remote one
  2. Keep local + conflict copy — Save the remote vault as a separate file
  3. Cancel — Abort the download

docs.title: Auto-Sync
docs.text:
pm cloud autosync

Runs periodic sync loops in the foreground. Useful for keeping vaults synchronized across active machines.

docs.title: Provider-Specific Sync
docs.text:
pm cloud sync gdrive
pm cloud sync github
pm cloud sync dropbox
pm cloud get gdrive
pm cloud get github

docs.title: Resetting Cloud Configuration
docs.text:
pm cloud reset

Clears all cloud provider credentials and configuration from the vault.

docs.title: Retrieval Keys
docs.text: When you first upload your vault, APM generates a retrieval key — a unique identifier that can be used to locate your vault on the provider. This is useful when setting up on a new device.

docs.title: Metadata Consent
docs.text:
For Google Drive and Dropbox, APM can store a one-way hash of your retrieval key in provider metadata. This enables vault lookup by key hash. You'll be asked for explicit consent:

  • If you consent: The vault can be found using the retrieval key hash
  • If you decline: The vault is identified by its provider-specific ID (Drive file ID, Dropbox path) instead

This ensures no identifying information is stored without your permission.

docs.title: Security Guarantees
docs.text:
What Is Uploaded

The cloud receives an encrypted binary blob — the same APMVAULT format stored on disk. It includes:

  • Encrypted vault data (using the vault's configured AEAD cipher)
  • HMAC-SHA256 integrity signature
  • Salt and profile metadata (in the unencrypted header)

What Is Never Uploaded

  • Your master password
  • Plaintext entries
  • Decrypted vault contents
  • Session data

docs.title: Using .apmignore with Cloud Sync
docs.text:
The .apmignore file is processed before encryption and upload. You can:

  • Exclude entire spaces
  • Exclude specific entries by space:type:name pattern
  • Use provider-specific rules
  • Strip vocabulary data

See the .apmignore Guide for full details.

docs.title: Conflict Resolution
docs.text:
When pm cloud get finds that the remote vault differs from your local vault:

  1. Overwrite local — The remote vault replaces your local vault entirely
  2. Keep local + save conflict copy — The remote vault is saved alongside your vault as a timestamped copy
  3. Cancel — No changes are made

Info

Conflict handling is whole-vault only. APM does not perform entry-level merge. This is intentional — merging encrypted data safely requires entry-level conflict detection, which could leak metadata about vault contents.

docs.title: "OAuth token expired"
docs.text: Re-run pm cloud init [provider] to re-authenticate.

docs.title: "Vault not found on provider"
docs.text: Either the vault was never uploaded, or the retrieval key/file ID has changed. Try uploading again with pm cloud sync.

docs.title: "HMAC verification failed after download"
docs.text: The downloaded vault may have been corrupted during transfer. Try downloading again. If the problem persists, the remote vault may have been tampered with.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Using .apmignore — Filter what gets uploaded
  • Sessions — Session management for sync workflows
  • Cloud Sync Concepts — Deep technical details

docs.title: Face ID (Optional)
docs.text:
Face ID enables biometric unlock using local face recognition. It is optional and only available when APM is built with the faceid build tag because it depends on native OpenCV and dlib libraries.

It is a convenience layer over the normal unlock flow, not a replacement for the master password. If Face ID is unavailable or verification fails, you can still unlock with your password.

docs.title: Build
docs.text:
# Standard build (no Face ID)
go build -o pm.exe

# Face ID build
go build -tags faceid -o pm.exe

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
pm faceid enroll
pm faceid status
pm faceid test
pm faceid remove

docs.title: pm faceid enroll
docs.text: Registers a local face profile for future unlock attempts.

docs.title: pm faceid status
docs.text: Shows whether Face ID enrollment metadata is present and available.

docs.title: pm faceid test
docs.text: Runs a verification check without changing enrollment.

docs.title: pm faceid remove
docs.text: Deletes the local Face ID enrollment metadata.

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
• Models are downloaded automatically to your user config directory under apm/faceid/models.
  • Enrollment metadata is stored next to the vault at faceid/enrollment.json.

docs.title: Typical Workflow
docs.text:
go build -tags faceid -o pm.exe
pm faceid enroll
pm faceid status
pm unlock

Face ID participates in the unlock path only when the binary was built with the faceid tag and enrollment data exists for the current vault.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Face ID uses the default camera (index 0).
  • If Face ID fails, you can always unlock with your master password.
  • Native OpenCV and dlib requirements are the reason this feature is behind a build tag.

docs.title: Importing and Exporting
docs.text: APM supports importing data from external sources and exporting your vault contents in multiple formats. This enables migration from other password managers and secure backup creation.

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text: Format Import Export Encryption Support Notes JSON ✅ ✅ ✅ Optional Full-fidelity, all entry types CSV ✅ ✅ ❌ Password entries only TXT ✅ ✅ ❌ Human-readable, optional redaction

docs.title: JSON Export
docs.text:
pm export json

Exports all vault entries to a JSON file with the full APM data structure. This is the most complete export format, preserving all entry types and their fields.

With encryption:

pm export json --encrypt

This encrypts the export file with a separate password, producing a protected JSON file that can only be read with the export password.

docs.title: CSV Export
docs.text:
pm export csv

Exports password entries only in a standard CSV format:

account,username,password
github.com,aarav,s3cr3t_p4ss

Warning

CSV export includes passwords in plaintext. Handle the output file with extreme care and delete it after use.

docs.title: TXT Export
docs.text:
pm export txt

Exports a human-readable text file covering all entry types with formatted sections.

With password redaction:

pm export txt --no-password

This creates a reference document with sensitive values replaced by ********.

docs.title: From JSON
docs.text:
pm import json backup.json

Imports entries from a JSON file matching APM's export format. All entry types are supported.

From encrypted JSON:

pm import json encrypted_backup.json --decrypt

You'll be prompted for the export password.

docs.title: From CSV
docs.text:
pm import csv passwords.csv

Expects columns for account, username, and password. This is compatible with exports from many other password managers (1Password, Bitwarden, Chrome, etc.).

docs.title: From TXT
docs.text:
pm import txt vault_export.txt

Parses structured text files. The parser looks for Account:, Username:, and Password: labels.

docs.title: Import Behavior
docs.text:
When importing:

  • Duplicate detection — APM checks for existing entries with the same account name
  • Space assignment — Imported entries are placed in the currently active space
  • Validation — Entries are validated against any loaded password policies
  • Audit logging — Each import action is recorded in the audit log

docs.title: From Chrome / Brave / Edge
docs.text:
1. Export passwords from chrome://settings/passwords (CSV format)
  2. Import: pm import csv chrome_passwords.csv

docs.title: From 1Password
docs.text:
1. Export from 1Password (CSV format)
  2. Import: pm import csv 1password_export.csv

docs.title: From Bitwarden
docs.text:
1. Export from Bitwarden (JSON or CSV)
  2. Import: pm import json bitwarden_export.json or pm import csv bitwarden_export.csv

docs.title: From KeePass
docs.text:
1. Export from KeePass (CSV format)
  2. Import: pm import csv keepass_export.csv

Tip

After importing, delete the export files from other password managers. They contain plaintext credentials.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Vault Management — Organizing imported entries
  • Cloud Sync — Sync your vault after importing

docs.title: Injecting Secrets Into Your Shell
docs.text:
pm inject exports vault data into the current shell session as environment variables. It is meant for short-lived developer workflows such as local app startup, CI smoke testing, and ad hoc scripting where a process expects secrets through env vars.

Unlike pm get, pm inject does not print the secret values directly for you to copy. It emits shell-specific export commands, and you evaluate those commands in your shell.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
pm inject:

  1. Unlocks the vault using your normal APM flow
  2. Resolves one or more vault entries by name
  3. Converts those entries into environment variables
  4. Stores a small local inject-session record so the variables can be removed later with pm inject kill

The command supports bash, zsh, fish, and PowerShell.

docs.title: Bash / Zsh
docs.text: eval "$(pm inject --inject github)"

docs.title: Fish
docs.text: eval (pm inject --inject github)

docs.title: PowerShell
docs.text:
pm inject --inject github | Invoke-Expression

After the command runs, the entry becomes an environment variable in the current shell.

For a password entry named github, the default variable name is:

GITHUB

docs.title: Selecting Entries
docs.text:
You can inject explicit entry names:

pm inject --inject github,aws-prod,openai-token

Entry lookup is by vault item name in the active space. If any requested entry is missing, the command fails rather than partially injecting.

Supported entry sources include:

  • Password entries
  • TOTP entries
  • Tokens
  • Secure notes
  • API keys
  • SSH keys
  • Wi-Fi passwords
  • Recovery codes
  • Certificates
  • Cloud credential secret keys
  • Docker registry tokens
  • SSH config private keys

docs.title: .apminject Files
docs.text:
If you omit --inject, APM searches upward from the current directory for a .apminject file.

This lets you keep project-local injection rules in the repo or workspace root.

docs.title: Simple List Format
docs.text:
- entry: github
- entry: aws-prod

docs.title: Wrapped Format
docs.text:
inject:
  - entry: github
  - entry: aws-prod

You can also use entries: instead of inject:.

docs.title: Custom Variable Names
docs.text:
inject:
  - entry: github
    as: GITHUB_TOKEN
  - entry: aws-prod
    as: AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

If as is omitted, APM derives a variable name automatically by uppercasing the entry name and replacing separators with underscores.

Examples:

  • github -> GITHUB
  • aws-prod -> AWS_PROD
  • openai token -> OPENAI_TOKEN

docs.title: Recommended Workflow
docs.text:
For one-off usage:

eval "$(pm inject --inject github)"

For project usage with a checked-in .apminject file:

eval "$(pm inject)"

When you are done:

eval "$(pm inject kill)"

On PowerShell:

pm inject kill | Invoke-Expression

pm inject kill removes the injected variables and clears the tracked inject session.

docs.title: setup-shell
docs.text:
APM can install a small shell helper so you do not have to type eval manually every time:

pm inject setup-shell

This adds an inject() helper to the detected shell config:

  • ~/.bashrc for Bash
  • ~/.zshrc for Zsh
  • ~/.config/fish/config.fish for Fish
  • $PROFILE for PowerShell

After reloading your shell config, you can run:

inject --inject github
inject
inject kill

docs.title: Session Behavior
docs.text:
APM tracks one active inject session at a time. If a session is already active, pm inject will stop and ask you to run pm inject kill first.

The session record stores:

  • A generated session ID
  • The injected variable names
  • Injection time
  • Parent shell PID

By default, this record lives under your user config directory:

<config>/apm/inject_session

If APM_DATA_DIR is set, APM stores it there instead.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Injected secrets live in your shell environment until you remove them or close the shell.
  • Child processes inherit those environment variables.
  • Prefer a dedicated shell for sensitive injection workflows.
  • Use pm inject kill immediately after the dependent process exits.
  • Do not commit .apminject files that reveal sensitive internal naming unless that is acceptable for the repo.

pm inject is convenient, but environment variables are still a wider exposure surface than keeping secrets only inside the vault.

docs.title: No .apminject file found
docs.text: Use --inject explicitly, or create a .apminject file in the current directory or a parent directory.

docs.title: Unknown shell warning
docs.text: APM falls back to Bash syntax if it cannot detect the shell from environment variables.

docs.title: PowerShell does not persist variables
docs.text:
Use:

pm inject --inject github | Invoke-Expression

Without Invoke-Expression, you will only print the export script instead of applying it.

docs.title: A session is already active
docs.text:
Run:

eval "$(pm inject kill)"

or on PowerShell:

pm inject kill | Invoke-Expression

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• pm unlock — Start the vault session required for secret access
  • pm get — View or copy a secret interactively
  • pm inject kill — Wipe injected variables from the active shell
  • pm inject setup-shell — Install the inject() shell helper

docs.title: MCP Integration
docs.text: APM includes a native Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that lets AI assistants like Claude Desktop, Cursor, and Windsurf interact with your vault — reading entries, searching credentials, retrieving TOTP codes, and even modifying vault contents with transaction guardrails.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant AI as AI Assistant
    participant MCP as APM MCP Server
    participant Vault as Encrypted Vault

    AI->>MCP: Request (with token)
    MCP->>MCP: Validate token + permissions
    MCP->>Vault: Decrypt & read (via active session)
    Vault-->>MCP: Vault data
    MCP-->>AI: Filtered response

The MCP server runs as a subprocess spawned by the AI client. It communicates via stdio transport and requires an active APM session (or ephemeral session) to access the vault.

docs.title: Step 1: Generate a Token
docs.text:
pm mcp token

You'll be prompted to:

  1. Name the token (e.g., "claude-desktop")
  2. Select permissions:
    • read — List and search entries (metadata only)
    • secrets — Decrypt and retrieve secret values
    • write — Add, edit, and delete entries
    • admin — Manage profiles, cloud config, view history and audit logs
  3. Set expiry (optional) — Token auto-expires after the specified duration

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Your AI Client
Step 3: Unlock Your Vault
docs.text:
pm unlock

Session Required

The MCP server requires an active APM session. You must run pm unlock before the AI agent can access the vault.

Alternatively, provide an ephemeral delegated session using APM_EPHEMERAL_ID:

pm session issue --label "mcp-claude" --scope read --ttl 1h
# Set APM_EPHEMERAL_ID in the MCP server config

docs.title: Permission Scopes
docs.text:
Tokens have fine-grained permission scopes that control exactly what the AI agent can do:

Scope Capabilities Tools read List entries, search, view metadata list_entries, search_entries, get_entry secrets Decrypt and retrieve secret values, get TOTP codes decrypt_entry, get_totp write Add, edit, delete entries, manage spaces add_entry, edit_entry, delete_entry, manage_spaces, install_plugin, cloud_sync admin Manage profiles, cloud config, view history/audit manage_profiles, cloud_config, get_history, get_audit_logs

docs.title: Transaction Guardrails
docs.text:
Write operations (add_entry, edit_entry, delete_entry) use a two-step transaction model to prevent unintended modifications:

sequenceDiagram
    participant AI as AI Assistant
    participant MCP as APM MCP Server

    AI->>MCP: add_entry (data)
    MCP-->>AI: Preview + tx_id
    Note over AI: AI reviews the preview
    AI->>MCP: add_entry (tx_id + approve: true)
    MCP-->>AI: Receipt ID (success)
  1. First call — Creates a preview transaction and returns a tx_id
  2. AI reviews — The AI (or user) reviews the preview
  3. Second call — Commits the transaction by sending tx_id + approve: true
  4. Receipt — A receipt ID is returned on successful commit

This prevents the AI from making irreversible changes without a confirmation step.

docs.title: Listing Tokens
docs.text:
pm mcp list

Shows all tokens with their names, permissions, creation dates, last usage, and usage counts.

docs.title: Revoking Tokens
docs.text:
pm mcp revoke "claude-desktop"

Revokes a token by name or token string. Revoked tokens are immediately rejected by the MCP server.

docs.title: Auto-Configuration
docs.text:
pm mcp config

Searches for known MCP client config files and offers to update them with your token automatically.

docs.title: Environment Variables for MCP
docs.text: Variable Purpose APM_VAULT_PATH Override the vault file location APM_EPHEMERAL_ID Use an ephemeral session instead of global APM_CONTEXT Set to mcp to indicate MCP context APM_ACTOR Identifies the actor in telemetry (e.g., AI)

docs.title: Security Best Practices
docs.text:
Least Privilege

Only grant the permissions your AI agent actually needs. For most use cases, read is sufficient. Only add secrets if the AI needs to retrieve actual passwords, and write only if you want the AI to manage entries.

Token Security

  • Store tokens securely — they grant vault access
  • Use token expiry for temporary access
  • Revoke tokens you no longer need
  • Use ephemeral sessions for additional binding (host, PID, agent)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• MCP Tools Reference — All tool schemas and permissions
  • MCP Server Concepts — Deep technical details
  • Sessions — Ephemeral session management

docs.title: Using Plugins
docs.text: APM features a manifest-based plugin system that lets you extend the CLI with custom commands, hooks, and automation workflows. Plugins are distributed via a cloud-based marketplace or installed locally.

docs.title: From the Marketplace
docs.text:
# Browse available plugins
pm plugins market

# Install a plugin by name
pm plugins install vault_cleaner

# Search for plugins
pm plugins search backup

docs.title: From a Local Directory
docs.text:
pm plugins local /path/to/plugin/directory

The directory must contain a valid plugin.json manifest.

docs.title: Listing Installed Plugins
docs.text: pm plugins installed

docs.title: Managing Plugin Permissions
docs.text: Every plugin declares the permissions it needs in its plugin.json manifest. APM enforces these at runtime and stores permission overrides inside your encrypted vault.

docs.title: Viewing Permissions
docs.text:
pm plugins access

Shows all installed plugins and their current permission states.

docs.title: Toggling Permissions
docs.text:
# Disable a specific permission for a plugin
pm plugins access vault_cleaner vault.delete off

# Re-enable it
pm plugins access vault_cleaner vault.delete on

Permission Overrides Travel with Your Vault

Permission overrides are stored inside the encrypted vault. When you sync your vault to cloud, the overrides travel with it — ensuring consistent security policy across devices.

docs.title: Running Plugin Commands
docs.text:
Plugins can register custom commands that appear in the APM CLI:

pm plugins run <plugin_name> <command>

Or, if the plugin registers hooks, they fire automatically during vault lifecycle events.

docs.title: Publishing Plugins
docs.text:
pm plugins push <plugin_name>

Uploads the plugin to the marketplace via your configured cloud provider. The plugin must have:

  • A valid plugin.json manifest
  • Version following semantic versioning (e.g., 1.0.0)

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
my_plugin/
└── plugin.json

docs.title: Manifest Schema (plugin.json)
docs.text:
{
  "name": "my_plugin",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "A brief description of what this plugin does",
  "author": "Your Name",
  "permissions_required": [
    "vault.read",
    "vault.write",
    "file.storage"
  ],
  "allowed_file_types": [".json", ".txt"],
  "commands": ["backup", "restore"],
  "hooks": ["post:add", "pre:sync"]
}

docs.title: Manifest Fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string ✅ Unique plugin identifier version string ✅ Semantic version (X.Y.Z) description string ❌ Human-readable description author string ❌ Plugin author permissions_required string[] ✅ List of required permissions allowed_file_types string[] ❌ File extensions the plugin may access commands string[] ❌ Commands the plugin exposes hooks string[] ❌ Lifecycle hooks to listen for

docs.title: Hook Types
docs.text:
Hooks fire at specific points in the vault lifecycle:

Hook Pattern When It Fires pre:add Before an entry is added post:add After an entry is added pre:sync Before a cloud sync post:sync After a cloud sync pre:delete Before an entry is deleted post:delete After an entry is deleted

docs.title: Example Plugins
docs.text: APM ships with example plugins in the examples/plugins/ directory:

docs.title: quick_backup — Automated Vault Backup
docs.text:
{
  "name": "quick_backup",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Creates a timestamped backup of the vault file",
  "permissions_required": ["vault.read", "vault.backup", "file.storage"],
  "commands": ["backup"]
}

docs.title: clip_auto_clear — Clipboard Auto-Clear
docs.text:
{
  "name": "clip_auto_clear",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Clears clipboard after a configurable delay",
  "permissions_required": ["system.clipboard.write"],
  "commands": ["clear"]
}

docs.title: cloud_sync_hook — Auto-Sync After Add
docs.text:
{
  "name": "cloud_sync_hook",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Triggers cloud sync after adding entries",
  "permissions_required": ["vault.read", "cloud.sync"],
  "hooks": ["post:add"]
}

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
Plugins Execute Code

Plugins can execute arbitrary commands on your system within their declared permissions. Always:

  1. Review permissions before installing (pm plugins access)
  2. Disable unnecessary permissions (pm plugins access <name> <perm> off)
  3. Only install plugins from trusted sources

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Plugin API Reference — Full permission catalog and step commands
  • Plugin Architecture — Deep technical details

docs.title: Managing Sessions
docs.text: APM uses explicit sessions as a security boundary — the vault is encrypted at rest and must be unlocked before any sensitive operation. Sessions prevent accidental long-term exposure of decrypted data.

docs.title: Session Lifecycle
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Locked: Vault at rest
    Locked --> Unlocked: pm unlock
    Unlocked --> Locked: pm lock
    Unlocked --> Locked: Session expired
    Unlocked --> Locked: Inactivity timeout
    Unlocked --> Active: Sensitive command
    Active --> Unlocked: Command completes

docs.title: Unlocking
docs.text:
pm unlock

You are prompted for:

  • Master password
  • Session duration — How long before the session auto-expires (default: 30 minutes)
  • Inactivity timeout — How long of idle time before auto-lock (default: 10 minutes)
  • Read-only mode — Optional flag that prevents all write operations

On success, APM:

  1. Decrypts the vault in memory
  2. Creates a session file in the system temp directory
  3. Starts a background cleanup process that deletes the session file when expired

docs.title: Locking
docs.text:
pm lock

Immediately:

  1. Deletes the session file
  2. Wipes decrypted data from memory
  3. Locks the autofill daemon (if running)

docs.title: Shell-Scoped Sessions
docs.text:
By default, APM uses a single global session. To run independent sessions per terminal, set the APM_SESSION_ID environment variable:

Bash / ZshPowerShell 
export APM_SESSION_ID="terminal1"
pm unlock
# This terminal has its own session

$env:APM_SESSION_ID = "terminal1"
pm unlock

Each shell with a different APM_SESSION_ID gets its own session file and can be unlocked/locked independently.

Session File Location

  • Global session: $TEMP/pm_session_global.json
  • Scoped session: $TEMP/pm_session_{SESSION_ID}.json

The session ID is sanitized to contain only alphanumeric characters.

docs.title: Ephemeral Delegated Sessions
docs.text: Ephemeral sessions are short-lived, context-bound tokens that delegate vault access to another process or agent without sharing your master password directly.

docs.title: Issuing an Ephemeral Session
docs.text:
pm session issue --label "CI Pipeline" --scope read --ttl 15m

Options:

Flag Description Default --label Human-readable label (none) --scope Access scope: read or write read --ttl Time to live (e.g., 15m, 1h) Required --bind-host Restrict to current machine false --bind-pid Restrict to a specific process ID (none) --agent Restrict to a specific agent name (none)

The command outputs an ephemeral session ID like eps_a1b2c3d4....

docs.title: Using an Ephemeral Session
docs.text:
Set the APM_EPHEMERAL_ID environment variable to use the delegated session:

export APM_EPHEMERAL_ID="eps_a1b2c3d4..."
pm get github  # Uses the ephemeral session instead of the regular one

docs.title: Security Bindings
docs.text:
Ephemeral sessions support three types of binding for additional security:

Binding What It Checks Host SHA-256 hash of hostname + username must match PID The calling process ID must match Agent The agent name (via APM_ACTOR) must match

If a binding mismatch is detected, the session is rejected.

docs.title: Managing Ephemeral Sessions
docs.text:
pm session list        # List all active ephemeral sessions
pm session revoke ID   # Revoke a specific session

Revoked and expired sessions are automatically cleaned up.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• CI/CD pipelines — Issue a short-lived read-only session for automated credential retrieval
  • MCP server — Provide an ephemeral session so the AI agent doesn't need your master password
  • Pair programming — Delegate temporary read access to a teammate's terminal

docs.title: How Sessions Interact with Other Features
docs.text: Feature Session Requirement pm add Active write session (not read-only) pm get Active session (any type) pm get edit/delete actions Active write session pm cloud sync Active session Autofill daemon Follows CLI session unlock/lock state MCP server Requires active session or ephemeral session

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• MCP Integration — Using ephemeral sessions with AI agents
  • Sessions Concept — Deep technical details

docs.title: Team Edition
docs.text: The APM Team Edition (pm-team) extends APM for organizational use — enabling secure credential sharing across teams with role-based access control, departmental isolation, and approval workflows.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
While the personal edition (pm) focuses on local-first individual security, the Team Edition adds:

  • Multi-user vault management with shared encryption
  • Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with configurable roles
  • Departments as isolated encryption domains
  • Approval workflows for gated access to sensitive entries
  • Health monitoring across the organization

docs.title: Initialize an Organization
docs.text:
pm-team init "Acme Corp"

This creates the organizational root environment with the organization name, initial admin user, and master encryption configuration.

docs.title: Adding Users
docs.text:
pm-team user add alice

Onboards a new member with a specified role. Users are assigned to departments and given role-based permissions.

docs.title: Listing Users
docs.text: pm-team user list

docs.title: Department Management
docs.text:
Departments provide isolated encryption domains within the organization:

# Create a department
pm-team dept create Engineering

# List departments
pm-team dept list

Each department has its own encryption context, meaning secrets in one department are cryptographically isolated from others.

docs.title: Entry Operations
docs.text:
Team entries work like personal entries but with organizational context:

# Add a shared entry
pm-team add

# Search for entries (optional query)
pm-team get [query]

# Edit an entry
pm-team edit [name]

# Delete an entry
pm-team del [name]

docs.title: Approval Workflows
docs.text:
Sensitive entries can require approval before access:

# List pending approvals
pm-team approvals list

# Approve or deny a request
pm-team approvals approve <request_id>
pm-team approvals deny <request_id>

The approval system ensures that high-sensitivity credentials are only accessed with explicit authorization from a manager or admin.

docs.title: Health Dashboard
docs.text:
pm-team health

Shows the organizational vault health status, including security scoring and vulnerability reporting across all departments.

docs.title: Security Architecture
docs.text:
The Team Edition uses the same cryptographic primitives as the personal edition:

  • Argon2id for key derivation
  • AEAD vault encryption aligned with the personal edition's cryptographic model
  • HMAC-SHA256 for integrity verification

docs.title: Key Differences
docs.text: Feature Personal Edition Team Edition Users Single Multiple with roles Encryption Single master key Per-department keys Access control Session-based RBAC + approval workflows Audit Local log Org-wide audit trail

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• RBAC and Roles — Role configuration details
  • Departments — Department isolation model
  • Approval Workflows — Configuring approval gates

docs.title: Generating TOTP Codes
docs.text: APM supports Time-Based One-Time Passwords (TOTP) for two-factor authentication. You can store TOTP secrets alongside your credentials, view live codes with countdown timers, and link them to the autofill daemon.

docs.title: Adding a TOTP Entry
docs.text:
pm add
# Select type: 2 (TOTP)
# Account: github.com
# Secret: JBSWY3DPEHPK3PXP  (your TOTP secret key)

Tip

Most services show the TOTP secret as a QR code during 2FA setup. Look for a "Can't scan? Show secret key" link to get the text secret for APM.

docs.title: Interactive List
docs.text:
pm totp

Opens an interactive view showing all your TOTP entries with:

  • Live codes that update every 30 seconds
  • Countdown timers showing time until the next code
  • Persistent ordering — reorder entries and the order is saved to your vault

Keyboard controls in the interactive TOTP view:

Key Action Enter Copy the selected code Up Move selection up Down Move selection down U Move entry up in the order D Move entry down in the order Q Quit the TOTP viewer

docs.title: Direct Copy
docs.text:
Copy a specific TOTP code without entering the interactive view:

pm totp github

This fuzzy-matches the entry name and copies the current code to your clipboard.

docs.title: TOTP Order Persistence
docs.text: When you reorder entries in the interactive TOTP view (using U and D keys), the ordering is persisted in your encrypted vault. This means your most-used 2FA accounts stay at the top across sessions and devices (via cloud sync).

docs.title: Linking TOTP to Autofill (Windows)
docs.text:
If you use the autofill daemon, you can link a TOTP entry to a domain so that fill sequences can include the OTP code:

pm autocomplete link-totp

This interactive command lets you select a TOTP entry and bind it to a domain/service. When the autofill daemon fills credentials for that domain, it can include the TOTP code in the sequence using the {TOTP} token.

docs.title: How TOTP Works
docs.text:
APM generates TOTP codes using the standard RFC 6238 algorithm:

  1. The TOTP secret is stored in the vault as a base32-encoded string
  2. The current Unix timestamp is divided into 30-second intervals
  3. An HMAC-SHA1 hash is computed using the secret and the interval counter
  4. A 6-digit code is extracted from the hash using dynamic truncation

The code refreshes every 30 seconds. APM's display includes a countdown timer so you know when to expect a new code.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Autofill on Windows — Set up autofill with TOTP linking
  • Vault Management — Managing all entry types

docs.title: Managing Your Vault
docs.text: This guide covers the day-to-day operations of working with your APM vault — adding entries, searching, editing, organizing with spaces, and using the vocabulary engine for notes.

docs.title: Interactive Add
docs.text:
pm add

This opens an interactive menu showing all 25+ supported secret types. Select a type, then fill in the structured fields. Each type has a specific schema — for example, an SSH Config entry asks for alias, host, user, port, key path, and fingerprint.

docs.title: Adding to a Specific Space
docs.text:
If you have spaces configured, entries inherit the currently active space. Switch spaces before adding:

pm space switch Work
pm add    # This entry will be in the "Work" space

docs.title: Field Validation
docs.text: APM validates fields as you enter them. If you have a password policy loaded, new passwords are validated against the policy's requirements (minimum length, uppercase, numbers, symbols).

docs.title: Fuzzy Search
docs.text:
pm get [query]

APM performs fuzzy search across all entry types simultaneously — matching against account names, usernames, service names, labels, and other identifiers. Results are ranked by match quality.

Search Examples

pm get github        # Find GitHub credentials
pm get aws           # Find AWS cloud credentials, API keys, etc.
pm get "john doe"    # Find contacts named John Doe
pm get wifi          # Find Wi-Fi passwords

docs.title: Interactive Browser
docs.text:
When results are returned, APM enters an interactive browser mode. Keyboard controls:

Key Action Enter View full entry details C Copy password/secret to clipboard E Edit this entry D Delete this entry S Toggle show/hide sensitive fields Q Quicklook (preview notes, render photos) M View metadata (timestamps, trust score) Esc Exit the browser

docs.title: Clipboard Expiry
docs.text: When you copy a secret to the clipboard, APM automatically clears it after a short timeout to prevent accidental exposure.

docs.title: Safe Display
docs.text:
Sensitive fields are hidden by default in search results. To reveal them:

pm get github --show-pass

Or press S in the interactive view.

docs.title: Editing and Deleting Entries
docs.text:
Entry mutation happens from the interactive pm get browser.

pm get github

From the results view:

  • Press E to edit the selected entry
  • Press D to delete the selected entry

Edits are tracked by the secret telemetry system, recording who modified the entry, when, and via what context. Deletions are recorded in the audit log.

Warning

Deletion is permanent. There is no trash or undo. If you need to preserve deleted entries, export your vault first.

docs.title: Organizing with Spaces
docs.text: Spaces provide logical segmentation within a single vault file. They're useful for separating work credentials from personal ones, or organizing by project.

docs.title: Creating and Managing Spaces
docs.text:
# Create a new space
pm space create DevOps

# List all spaces
pm space list

# Switch to a space
pm space switch DevOps

# See the currently active space
pm space current

# Remove a space (entries in it are moved to default)
pm space remove DevOps

docs.title: How Spaces Affect Operations
docs.text:
• Adding — New entries inherit the active space
  • Searching — pm get searches within the active space by default
  • Cloud sync — Spaces can be excluded via .apmignore
  • TOTP — TOTP entries are space-aware

docs.title: Generating Passwords
docs.text:
pm gen

APM generates a high-entropy password using cryptographically secure randomness. The generated password is displayed and automatically copied to your clipboard.

Tip

Generate a password first with pm gen, then paste it when pm add asks for the password field.

docs.title: Notes and the Vocabulary Engine
docs.text: Secure notes in APM are more than text blobs. The vocabulary engine builds a compressed index from your notes and provides autocomplete functionality.

docs.title: Enabling Vocabulary
docs.text:
pm vocab enable     # Turn on vocabulary indexing
pm vocab disable    # Turn it off
pm vocab status     # Check if it's active

docs.title: Writing Notes with Autocomplete
docs.text: When adding or editing a secure note, APM offers autocomplete suggestions based on your vocabulary. Suggestions are ranked by frequency and user feedback (accepted suggestions increase a word's score; dismissed ones decrease it).

docs.title: Managing the Vocabulary
docs.text:
# List all indexed words with scores
pm vocab

# Create an alias (normalizes terms)
pm vocab alias k8s kubernetes
pm vocab alias tf terraform

# List all aliases
pm vocab alias-list

# Remove an alias
pm vocab alias-remove k8s

# Adjust word ranking manually
pm vocab rank deploy +5    # Boost "deploy"
pm vocab rank temp -3      # Demote "temp"

# Remove a word from the index
pm vocab remove obsolete_term

# Rebuild the entire vocabulary from current notes
pm vocab reindex

docs.title: How Vocabulary Storage Works
docs.text:
The vocabulary is stored as a gzip-compressed JSON blob inside the encrypted vault (Vault.VocabCompressed). This means:

  • It's encrypted at rest alongside all other vault data
  • It can be stripped from cloud uploads using ignore:vocab in .apmignore
  • It's rebuilt from notes content during reindexing

docs.title: Health Dashboard
docs.text:
pm health

Produces a numeric score (0–100) based on:

  • Encryption profile — Hardened/Paranoid profiles earn bonus points; Legacy loses points
  • Alert configuration — Enabled alerts earn points
  • Weak secrets — Passwords shorter than 8 characters are penalized
  • High-risk secrets — Trust scores below 55 (high risk) or 35 (critical) add penalties

docs.title: Trust Scores
docs.text:
pm trust

Every secret gets a trust score based on:

Factor Penalty Threshold Marked as exposed −45 Any No rotation > 365 days −35 Last rotation too old No rotation > 180 days −20 Last rotation aging No rotation > 90 days −10 Last rotation recent Access count > 200 −20 High frequency Access count > 75 −10 Elevated frequency Privilege: critical −15 Root/admin secrets Privilege: elevated −8 Elevated access

Risk levels: Low (≥80), Medium (≥55), High (≥35), Critical (<35)

docs.title: Audit Log
docs.text:
pm audit

Every vault interaction is logged with:

  • Timestamp — When the action occurred
  • Action — What was done (e.g., ENTRY_ADDED, VAULT_UNLOCKED, CLOUD_SYNCED)
  • User — System username
  • Hostname — Machine name

The audit log is stored outside the vault at ~/.config/apm/audit.json and is append-only.

docs.title: Cryptographic Information
docs.text:
pm cinfo

Displays the active cryptographic parameters:

  • Profile name and Argon2id parameters
  • Nonce sizes
  • Vault format version
  • Whether recovery is configured

docs.title: .apmignore Reference
docs.text: Complete format specification for the .apmignore file that controls cloud upload filtering.

docs.title: File Resolution
docs.text:
APM searches for .apmignore in this order:

  1. Same directory as the vault file
  2. Current working directory

The first file found is used. Only one .apmignore file is loaded per sync operation.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
• Lines starting with # are comments
  • Empty lines are ignored
  • Section headers are enclosed in []
  • Patterns support * glob wildcards

docs.title: [spaces]
docs.text:
List space names to exclude from uploads. One per line.

[spaces]
private
temp_*
archive
Pattern Matches private Exactly "private" temp_* "temp_1", "temp_staging", etc. * All spaces (upload nothing)

docs.title: [entries]
docs.text:
Entry patterns in space:type:name format.

[entries]
work:password:legacy_admin
*:notes:*draft*
personal:ssh_key:*
*:*:test_*
Component Description Wildcard space Space name * = any space type Entry type * = any type name Entry name * = any name

Type identifiers: password, totp, token, notes, api_key, ssh_key, ssh_config, wifi, gov_id, medical, travel, contact, cloud_creds, k8s, docker, cicd, recovery_codes, certificate, banking, document, software_license, legal, photo, audio, video

# ============================================
# .apmignore — Cloud Upload Filter
# ============================================

# Never sync these spaces
[spaces]
private
temp_*

# Exclude specific entries from all providers
[entries]
work:password:local_dev_db
*:notes:*scratch*
*:ssh_key:id_ed25519_local

# Provider-specific exclusions
[cloud-specific-ignore]
dropbox:work:password:legacy_admin

ignore:vocab

docs.title: pm setup
docs.text:
Run the guided setup flow for a new or existing vault.

pm setup
pm setup --non-interactive

pm setup creates the vault directory structure, initializes a vault if none exists, recommends a profile based on detected hardware, asks for the encryption method for new vaults, then walks through spaces, plugins, and cloud sync.

docs.title: pm unlock
docs.text:

Prompts for master password, session duration, inactivity timeout, and optional read-only mode. Also unlocks the autofill daemon if running.

docs.title: pm lock

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: All environment variables supported by APM.

docs.title: Core Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Default APM_VAULT_PATH Override the vault file location ./vault.dat APM_SESSION_ID Scope sessions to a specific shell instance global APM_EPHEMERAL_ID Use an ephemeral delegated session instead of the global (none)

docs.title: Context Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Default APM_ACTOR Identifies the actor in telemetry and audit logs (system user) APM_CONTEXT Execution context identifier (e.g., mcp, ci) (none)

docs.title: APM_VAULT_PATH
docs.text:
Overrides the default vault file location for all commands:

export APM_VAULT_PATH="/secure/vault.dat"
pm unlock  # Uses /secure/vault.dat instead of ./vault.dat

docs.title: APM_SESSION_ID
docs.text:
Creates independent sessions per terminal. Each unique value gets its own session file:

# Terminal 1
export APM_SESSION_ID="dev"
pm unlock

# Terminal 2
export APM_SESSION_ID="ops"
pm unlock
# Both terminals have independent sessions

Session file naming: $TEMP/pm_session_{APM_SESSION_ID}.json

docs.title: APM_EPHEMERAL_ID
docs.text:
Uses a previously issued ephemeral session for authentication:

export APM_EPHEMERAL_ID="eps_a1b2c3d4..."
pm get github  # Authenticated via ephemeral session

When set, APM validates the ephemeral session's bindings (host, PID, agent) instead of checking the regular session file.

docs.title: APM_ACTOR
docs.text:
Tags audit log entries and telemetry with the actor name:

export APM_ACTOR="github-actions"
pm get aws_credentials  # Audit log shows actor = "github-actions"

Used by ephemeral sessions to verify agent binding.

docs.title: APM_CONTEXT
docs.text:
Identifies the execution context:

export APM_CONTEXT="mcp"  # Marks operations as MCP-initiated
export APM_CONTEXT="ci"   # Marks operations as CI/CD-initiated

Affects telemetry recording (e.g., last_accessor field in secret telemetry).

docs.title: MCP Tools Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for every tool exposed by the APM MCP server, including schemas, required permissions, and transaction behavior.

docs.title: list_entries
docs.text:
List all entries in the vault (metadata only).

Required scope: read

Returns: Array of entry summaries with IDs, names, types, spaces, and creation dates. Sensitive fields (passwords, keys) are not included.

docs.title: search_entries
docs.text:
Search entries by query string.

Required scope: read

Parameters:

Parameter Type Required Description query string ✅ Search query (fuzzy) space string ❌ Filter by space type string ❌ Filter by entry type

Returns: Matching entries with relevance scoring.

docs.title: get_entry
docs.text:
Get a specific entry by ID or name.

Required scope: read (metadata) or secrets (sensitive fields)

Parameters:

Parameter Type Required Description id string ❌ Entry UUID name string ❌ Entry name (fuzzy match) include_secret bool ❌ Include sensitive fields

Notes: If include_secret is true, the secrets scope is required. Without it, passwords, keys, and tokens are redacted.

docs.title: decrypt_entry
docs.text:

Required scope: secrets

Parameters:

Parameter Type Required Description id string ✅ Entry UUID

docs.title: get_totp
docs.text:

Required scope: secrets

Parameters:

Parameter Type Required Description account string ✅ TOTP account name

Returns: Current 6-digit TOTP code and seconds remaining.

docs.title: Write Tools
docs.text: All write tools use transaction guardrails.

docs.title: add_entry
docs.text:
Add a new entry to the vault.

Required scope: write

Parameters:

Parameter Type Required Description type string ✅ Entry type fields object ✅ Entry fields (type-specific) space string ❌ Target space tx_id string ❌ Transaction ID (for commit) approve bool ❌ Commit flag

Transaction flow:

  1. First call (no tx_id) → Returns preview + tx_id
  2. Second call (tx_id + approve: true) → Commits and returns receipt ID

docs.title: Transaction Guardrails Summary
docs.text: Tool Uses Transaction Preview Phase Commit Phase add_entry ✅ Returns preview + tx_id Requires tx_id + approve: true edit_entry ✅ Returns preview + tx_id Requires tx_id + approve: true delete_entry ✅ Returns preview + tx_id Requires tx_id + approve: true manage_spaces ❌ — Executes immediately All read tools ❌ — Executes immediately

docs.text: Code Description PERMISSION_DENIED Token lacks required scope SESSION_REQUIRED No active APM session ENTRY_NOT_FOUND Entry ID/name not found TRANSACTION_EXPIRED tx_id expired or already committed INVALID_PARAMETERS Missing or malformed parameters VAULT_LOCKED Vault is not unlocked

docs.title: Step Commands
docs.text:
The step executor supports these built-in commands:

Command Required Permission Description vault.list vault.read List vault entries vault.get vault.read Get a specific entry vault.add vault.write Add a new entry vault.edit vault.write Edit an existing entry vault.delete vault.delete Delete an entry vault.search vault.read Search entries file.read file.storage Read a file file.write file.storage Write a file file.delete file.storage Delete a file file.list file.storage List files exec system.exec Execute shell command http.get network.http HTTP GET request http.post network.http HTTP POST request http.download network.http Download a file crypto.hash crypto.hash Compute SHA-256 hash crypto.random crypto.random Generate random bytes set (none) Set a variable print (none) Output text prompt ui.prompt Ask user for input confirm ui.confirm Ask yes/no question toast ui.toast Show notification clipboard system.clipboard.* Copy to clipboard

docs.title: Variable Substitution
docs.text:
Steps can reference variables from previous steps:

${variable_name}    → Value of the variable
${output.0}         → First line of previous step output

docs.title: Hook Patterns
docs.text:
pre:<command>   — Fires before the command executes
post:<command>  — Fires after the command completes

docs.title: Available Hooks
docs.text:
Hook Fires When pre:add Before an entry is added post:add After an entry is added pre:edit Before an entry is edited post:edit After an entry is edited pre:delete Before an entry is deleted post:delete After an entry is deleted pre:sync Before cloud sync post:sync After cloud sync pre:unlock Before vault unlock post:unlock After vault unlock pre:lock Before vault lock post:lock After vault lock pre:export Before export post:export After export pre:import Before import post:import After import

If a pre: hook returns an error, the associated command is aborted.

docs.title: Runtime Permission Overrides
docs.text:
Users can override plugin permissions after installation:

# Interactive toggle list (space key to select/deselect)
pm plugins access

# Direct toggle
pm plugins access <plugin> <permission> on|off

Overrides are stored inside the encrypted vault under plugin_permission_overrides and synced across devices.

The effective permissions for a plugin are:

effective = declared_permissions ∩ user_overrides

If a user disables a permission, the plugin cannot use it regardless of what the manifest declares.

docs.title: Policies Reference
docs.text: YAML policy schema for the APM policy engine.

docs.title: Schema
docs.text:
name: string (required)
description: string (optional)

password_policy:
  min_length: integer (default: 8)
  require_uppercase: boolean (default: false)
  require_numbers: boolean (default: false)
  require_symbols: boolean (default: false)

rotation_policy:
  rotate_every_days: integer (default: 0, 0 = disabled)
  notify_before_days: integer (default: 0)

classification:
  entry_name: privilege_level
  # privilege_level: critical | root | admin | elevated | normal

docs.title: password_policy
docs.text: Field Type Default Description min_length int 8 Minimum characters require_uppercase bool false At least one uppercase letter (A-Z) require_numbers bool false At least one digit (0-9) require_symbols bool false At least one special character

docs.title: rotation_policy
docs.text: Field Type Default Description rotate_every_days int 0 Rotation interval (0 = disabled) notify_before_days int 0 Pre-rotation notification window

docs.title: classification
docs.text:
Maps entry names to privilege levels. Used by the trust scoring system:

Level Trust Penalty Description critical −15 Highest privilege root −15 Root-level access admin −12 Administrative access elevated −8 Above-normal privilege normal 0 Standard access

docs.title: Loading Policies
docs.text:
pm policy load ./policies/

Loads all .yaml and .yml files from the directory. Multiple files are merged in filesystem order.

docs.text:

password_policy:
  min_length: 12

description: Corporate security standard

  require_uppercase: true
  require_numbers: true
  require_symbols: true

  rotate_every_days: 60
  notify_before_days: 14

  aws_root_key: critical
  production_db: critical
  staging_api: elevated
  ci_token: normal

docs.title: Storage Reference
docs.text: File locations and data layout for all APM artifacts.

docs.title: Vault File
docs.text:
Item Location Default vault ./vault.dat (current working directory) Override APM_VAULT_PATH environment variable

The vault is a single binary file in V4 format containing all encrypted entries, configuration, cloud tokens, MCP tokens, plugin overrides, vocabulary, and telemetry.

docs.title: Session Files
docs.text:
File Purpose $TEMP/pm_session_global.json Global session (default) $TEMP/pm_session_{SESSION_ID}.json Shell-scoped session

Session files contain:

  • Session ID
  • Unlock/expiry timestamps
  • Inactivity timeout
  • Read-only flag
  • Hashed master password

docs.title: Ephemeral Session Store
docs.text:
File Purpose $TEMP/.apm_ephemeral_sessions.json All ephemeral sessions

Contains an array of ephemeral session objects with IDs, bindings, expiry, and revocation status.

docs.title: Audit Log
docs.text:
File Purpose ~/.config/apm/audit.json Tamper-evident audit log

Append-only log of vault interactions with timestamps, actions, users, and hostnames.

docs.title: Autofill State
docs.text:
File Purpose $TEMP/.apm_autofill_state.json Daemon PID, port, and token

Created when the autofill daemon starts. Contains the PID, loopback address, bearer token, and start time.

docs.title: Plugin Directory
docs.text: Location Purpose ~/.config/apm/plugins/ Installed plugin directories ~/.config/apm/plugins/<name>/plugin.json Plugin manifest

docs.title: Cloud Configuration
docs.text: Cloud provider credentials (OAuth tokens, PATs) are stored inside the encrypted vault — not in separate files. This ensures they're protected by the same encryption and travel with the vault during sync.

docs.title: .apmignore
docs.text: Location Purpose Same directory as vault file Primary location Current working directory Fallback location

docs.title: Policy Files
docs.text:
Policy files are loaded on demand from a user-specified directory. They are not persisted in the vault.

pm policy load ./policies/

docs.title: Temporary Files
docs.text:
Pattern Purpose $TEMP/apm_export_* Export files $TEMP/apm_recovery_* Recovery ceremony data

Temporary files are cleaned up after use.

docs.title: Team Edition
docs.text: APM Team Edition (pm-team) extends the personal password manager for organizational use with multi-user support, role-based access control, departmental isolation, and approval workflows.

docs.title: In This Section
docs.text:
• RBAC and Roles — Role-based access control system
  • Departments — Isolated encryption domains
  • Approval Workflows — Gated access to sensitive credentials

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Team Edition shares the same cryptographic foundation as the personal edition (Argon2id + AEAD vault encryption + HMAC-SHA256) but adds an organizational layer:

Feature Personal (pm) Team (pm-team) Users Single user Multiple users with roles Vault encryption Single master key Per-department keys Access control Session-based RBAC + approval workflows Audit scope Local machine Organization-wide Entry sharing Not applicable Controlled sharing via roles

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Initialize an organization
pm-team init "Acme Corp"

# Create departments
pm-team dept create Engineering
pm-team dept create Finance

# Add users
pm-team user add alice --role admin --dept Engineering
pm-team user add bob --role member --dept Finance

# Add shared entries
pm-team add

# Search entries
pm-team get [query]

# Check organizational health
pm-team health

docs.title: Approval Workflows
docs.text: The approval system gates access to sensitive credentials, ensuring that high-value secrets are only accessed with explicit authorization from an authorized approver.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Member
    participant APM
    participant Manager

    Member->>APM: Request access to sensitive entry
    APM->>APM: Check if approval required
    APM-->>Member: Access pending approval
    APM->>Manager: Notification of pending request
    Manager->>APM: pm-team approvals approve <id>
    APM-->>Member: Access granted

docs.title: Viewing Pending Requests
docs.text:
pm-team approvals list

Shows all pending approval requests with:

  • Request ID
  • Requester name and role
  • Entry requested
  • Timestamp
  • Status

docs.title: Approving a Request
docs.text: pm-team approvals approve <request_id>

docs.title: Denying a Request
docs.text: pm-team approvals deny <request_id>

docs.title: Who Can Approve
docs.text: Role Can Approve Owner ✅ Admin ✅ Manager ✅ Member ❌ Viewer ❌

docs.title: Audit Trail
docs.text:
All approval actions (requests, approvals, denials) are recorded in the organizational audit log with:

  • Timestamp
  • Action (APPROVAL_REQUESTED, APPROVAL_GRANTED, APPROVAL_DENIED)
  • Requester
  • Approver
  • Entry details

docs.title: Departments
docs.text: Departments provide isolated encryption domains within the organization. Each department has its own encryption context, ensuring secrets in one department are cryptographically isolated from others.

docs.title: Creating Departments
docs.text:
pm-team dept create Engineering
pm-team dept create Finance
pm-team dept create Operations

docs.title: Managing Departments
docs.text:
# List all departments
pm-team dept list

# View department details
pm-team dept info Engineering

docs.title: Isolation Model
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph Org["Organization: Acme Corp"]
        subgraph Eng["Engineering"]
            A[SSH Keys]
            B[API Tokens]
            C[Cloud Creds]
        end
        subgraph Fin["Finance"]
            D[Banking Creds]
            E[Payment APIs]
        end
        subgraph Ops["Operations"]
            F[Server Access]
            G[Monitoring Keys]
        end
    end

Each department:

  • Has its own encryption key derived from the organizational master
  • Has members who are assigned via roles
  • Contains entries visible only to its members
  • Maintains its own audit trail

docs.title: Cross-Department Access
docs.text:
By default, entries in one department are invisible to members of other departments. Cross-department access requires:

  1. An admin or owner to grant it explicitly
  2. Or an approval workflow to be triggered

This ensures that even within the same organization, credential isolation is enforced at the cryptographic level.

docs.title: RBAC and Roles
docs.text: The Team Edition uses Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to manage permissions across the organization.

docs.title: Role Hierarchy
docs.text:
graph TB
    A[Owner] --> B[Admin]
    B --> C[Manager]
    C --> D[Member]
    D --> E[Viewer]
Role Capabilities Owner Full control — manage org, departments, users, all entries Admin Manage departments, users, entries within assigned departments Manager Manage entries, approve access requests within department Member Read/write entries within department, request approval for others Viewer Read-only access to department entries

docs.title: Assigning Roles
docs.text:
# Add user with a role
pm-team user add alice --role admin --dept Engineering

# Change a user's role
pm-team user role alice manager

docs.text: Action Owner Admin Manager Member Viewer Create departments ✅ ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌ Add users ✅ ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌ Assign roles ✅ ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌ Add entries ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ ❌ Read entries ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ Edit entries ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ ❌ Delete entries ✅ ✅ ✅ ❌ ❌ Approve requests ✅ ✅ ✅ ❌ ❌ View audit logs ✅ ✅ ✅ ❌ ❌ Manage cloud config ✅ ✅ ❌ ❌ ❌
